coredata.dc:title: A signal-on fluorescent sensor for ultra-trace detection of Hg2+ via Ag+ mediated sulfhydryl functionalized carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Yong
Shi, Limei
Luo, Xueli
Chen, Xiumei
Yang, Weixia
Tang, Wenzhi
Wang, Jianlong
Yue, Tianli
Li, Zhonghong
coredata.dc:description: Sulfhydryl functionalized carbon dots (HS-CDs) mediated by Ag+ were used to develop a fluorescent sensor for the determination of Hg2+ in aqueous solutions. FTIR and XPS confirmed the successful modification of -SH on the surface of HS-CDs. Dripping Ag+ into HS-CDs yielded brown agglomerates of silver thiolate leading to strong FL quenching of the HS-CDs. The signal-on fluorescent sensor was acheived by dispersing the precipitates in a PBS buffer (pH 7.4, 0.01 mol/L). Remarkable FL recovery was observed upon the addition of Hg2+ into the sensor owing to the energy trap induced by exposure of -NH2 on the surface of the HS-CDs. Fluorescence intensities of the HS-CDs were linearly recovered in Hg2+ concentrations of 0.01–0.75 nmol/L with an extremely low detection limit of 4.2 pmol/L. The sensor exhibited stronger specificity toward Hg2+ than co-existent metal ions. The detecting results for real water samples obtained by the sensor showed good agreement with ICP-AES (P > 0.05), demonstrating a sensitive and selective fluorescent nanosensor for ultra-trace determination of Hg2+ with acceptable precision and satisfactory accuracy.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the HS-CDs/Ag FL sensor for Hg2+. (A) Absorption spectrum of the HS-CDs/Ag precipitate, the insets are corresponding photos of HS-CDs/Ag under visible light (left) and UV light at 365 nm (right). (B) FL spectra of HS-CDs/Ag and HS-CDs/Ag + Hg2+, the insets are corresponding photos of the HS-CDs/Ag sensor (left) and the HS-CDs/Ag + Hg2+ (right) under UV light at 365 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) TEM image of the HS-CDs, the inset is particle size distribution of the HS-CDs. (B) FTIR spectra of citric acid, l-cysteine and the HS-CDs. (C) XPS spectra of HS-CDs, HS-CDs/Ag, HS-CDs/Ag + Hg2+. (D) UV–vis absorption, FL excitation and emission spectra of HS-CDs, the insets are corresponding photographs of HS-CDs under visible light (left) and a UV light of 365 nm (right). (E) FL intensities of HS-CDs versus emission wavelengths and excitation wavelengths. (F) FL intensities of HS-CDs versus emission wavelengths and different pHs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of HS-CDs, HS-CDs/Ag and AgNO3 solution and FL excitation and emission spectrum of HS-CD, the insets are corresponding photographs of agglomerates observed under visual light (a) and UV lamp at 365 nm (b). (B) FTIR spectra of HS-CDs, HS-CDs/Ag and Hg2+ addition of HS-CDs/Ag. (C) High resolution XPS spectrum of S1s. (D) High resolution XPS spectrum of Ag3d. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) FL intensities of the HS-CDs versus emission wavelengths and different Hg2+concentrations in the range of 0–2.00 nmol/L (B) FL recovery intensities of the HS-CDs/Ag against Hg2+ concentrations in the range of 0–2.00 nmol/L. The inset is the corresponding regression line in the concentration range of 0–0.75 nmol/L. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) High resolution XPS spectra of S1s in HS-CDs, HS-CDs/Ag and HS-CDs/Ag + Hg2+. (B) High resolution XPS spectrum of Ag 3d. (C) High resolution XPS spectrum of Hg 4f. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Specificity of HS-CDs/Ag probe toward Hg2+. (B) FL recovery intensities of the sensor toward Hg2+ and 100-fold different co-existing metal ions. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Recovery tests of Hg2+ in water samples.

head.ce:title: A signal-on fluorescent sensor for ultra-trace detection of Hg2+ via Ag+ mediated sulfhydryl functionalized carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Agro-products (Yangling), Ministry of Agriculture, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Quality & Safety Risk Assessment for Agro-products (Yangling)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center of Agriculture Integration Test (Yangling), Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Agriculture Integration Test (Yangling)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulfhydryl functionalized carbon dots (HS-CDs) mediated by Ag+ were used to develop a fluorescent sensor for the determination of Hg2+ in aqueous solutions. FTIR and XPS confirmed the successful modification of -SH on the surface of HS-CDs. Dripping Ag+ into HS-CDs yielded brown agglomerates of silver thiolate leading to strong FL quenching of the HS-CDs. The signal-on fluorescent sensor was acheived by dispersing the precipitates in a PBS buffer (pH 7.4, 0.01 mol/L). Remarkable FL recovery was observed upon the addition of Hg2+ into the sensor owing to the energy trap induced by exposure of -NH2 on the surface of the HS-CDs. Fluorescence intensities of the HS-CDs were linearly recovered in Hg2+ concentrations of 0.01–0.75 nmol/L with an extremely low detection limit of 4.2 pmol/L. The sensor exhibited stronger specificity toward Hg2+ than co-existent metal ions. The detecting results for real water samples obtained by the sensor showed good agreement with ICP-AES (P > 0.05), demonstrating a sensitive and selective fluorescent nanosensor for ultra-trace determination of Hg2+ with acceptable precision and satisfactory accuracy.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial and agricultural waste disposal has given rise to toxic heavy metal contamination on a global scale [1,2]. Mercury (Hg) presents severe threats to human health due to its high toxicity and easy bioaccumulation via inhalation and skin absorption [3,4]. The United State Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) has enacted an extremely strict standard for Hg2+ in drinking water with a recommended allowable maximum concentration of 10 nmol/L [5]. Atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrum (ICP-MS), and inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [6–9] have been used to detect Hg2+. These testing methods require tedious, time-consuming procedures and high expertise. They are particularly high-cost, limiting extensive application. Therefore, rapid response, sensitive and convenient analytical methods are urgently needed for the determination of trace Hg2+ to monitor mercury contamination.

Optical methods possess good potential to detect metal ions effectively and rapidly [10]. Infrared (IR) spectrometry, ultraviolet (UV) spectrometry and fluorescence (FL) spectrometry are suitable for rapid and real-time detection of metal ions. The fluorescent techniques have been increasingly applied to detect metal ions owing to their simplicity, high sensitivity and low-cost [11–13]. The flourishing of nanomaterial research has provided numerous novel FL sensors for various analytes. Quantum dots (QDs) are superior in optical and electrical properties compared with conventional organic FL dyes [14–17]. Extensive use of heavy metal-based QDs is limited because of potential threats to the environment and to operators [17,18]. Therefore, heavy metal-free FL nanosensors are urgently needed for this type of research. Carbon dots (CDs) are regarded as novel and eco-friendly QDs. They have drawn tremendous attention as FL sensors due to their superb attributes which include ease of preparation, splendid chemical inertness, superior anti-photo-bleaching, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [14,15,19]. CDs-based FL sensors for metal ions mainly rely on electron transfer (ET) from the chelation between the metal ions and the surface groups of CDs to create a change in FL intensity [20]. The sensitivity and the selectivity of these FL sensors are dominated by the affinity of active groups (e.g. -NH2 and -SH) on the CDs surface toward the targeted metal ions [19,21]. Sulfur/Nitrogen co-doped CDs with high quantum yield provide sulfur and nitrogen atoms to fabricate the active groups [22,23], which is crucial to recognize targets specially. Thereby, the functionalized CDs are frequently used as FL sensors for label-free detection of Hg2+ [5,24,25] through FL quenching caused by rapid chelation between the active groups and Hg2+. However, the specificity towards Hg2+ of the sensors based on direct FL quenching encounters serious challenges because of the random coordination between various metal ions and the active groups [26]. A “mediated” FL sensor with an intermediate as quencher possesses good potential to overcome the shortcoming. Using a switchable fluorescent sensor is a useful approach to discreetly recognize target analytes [27]. Analytes, especially the organic molecular, can capture the quencher from quenched CDs to restore the FL owing to their higher affinity with the intermediate. Switchable fluorescent sensors have already been assembled to detect organic molecules using metal ions as intermediates to quench CDs [27–29]. Compared to the detection of organic molecules using metal ions-quenched CDs, the application of metal ions-quenched CDs for metal ion detection has been rarely reported. A reported switchable fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ detection employed ligand-metal binding to induce the FL changes [30]. Unfortunately, ligands with similar active groups are liable to interact with metal ions randomly, resulting in lower selectivity and sensitivity of the sensors toward Hg2+.

Metallothioneins (MTs), cysteine-rich proteins, can chelate heavy metal ions by coordinating sulfhydryl groups (-SH) of cysteine residues [31,32]. Ag+ can chelate MTs resulting in agglomerates of MTs as well as color change because of the formation of silver-thiolate precipitates [33,34]. It is assumed that Ag+ induces agglomeration of -SH/-NH2-enriched CDs and quenches the FL due to the change of the surface states of CDs. Subsequently, Hg2+ could be substituted for Ag+ to form a new coordination compound such as Hg-cysteine [36] because the binding energy barrier of Hg2+ to -SH (−234.7 kcal/mol) is lower than Ag+ (−164.8 kcal/mol) [35]. The higher affinity between -SH and Hg2+ should lead to the substitution of Hg2+ for Ag+ in the agglomerate of CDs/Ag, and the rapid formation of amalgam might enhance the selectivity and sensitivity of the CDs based sensors providing a promising FL assay to detect Hg2+. In both cases the formation of amalgam reduces the free Hg2+ and Ag+ ions available to re-chelate the CDs and thus avoids re-quenching the FL.

Sulfhydryl functionalized CDs (HS-CDs) were readily synthesized to fabricate a fluorescent sensor via Ag+ mediating for Hg2+ detection. The FL of CDs can be quenched by Ag+ via the formation of HS-CDs/Ag agglomerate. The signal-on FL sensor was obtained by dispersing HS-CDs/Ag in a PBS buffer and the FL of the sensor could be recovered by Hg2+. The substitution of Hg2+ for Ag+ in HS-CDs/Ag and subsequent amalgam generation endowed the sensor with an ultra-low detection limit toward Hg2+. The development of the signal-on fluorescent sensor is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The sensor showed excellent performance for detecting Hg2+ in real water samples and had a good agreement with ICP-AES, demonstrating its feasibility and practical application for determination of Hg2+ in water.

Citric acid (CA, 99.9%) was obtained from Tianjin Bodi Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). l-cysteine (L-cys, 97.0%) was obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Mercury nitrate monohydrate (H2HgN2O7), silver nitrate (AgNO3) and other reagents were obtained from Tianjin Chemical Reagents Factory. All chemicals were analytical grade and were used without further purification. The dialysis bag (MWCO 100–500 Da) and polyether sulfonic (PES) membrane (0.22 μm) were obtained from Beijing Solabio Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Deionized water (DW) was used throughout the experimental procedures.

The HS-CDs were prepared according to a previous report [38] with certain modifications. They were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal treatment using CA and L-cys as precursors. Applying our preliminary study 1 g of CA and 0.25 g of L-cys were dissolved in 50 mL DW under ultrasonic dissolution for 10 min, and the solution was treated hydrothermally in a Teflon-equipped stainless-steel auto-clave at 180 °C for 2 h. The resultant solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove precipitates then filtered through a 0.22 μm PES membrane and dialyzed for 24 h in DW. Lyophilized HS-CDs were dissolved in DW to a concentration of 2.0 mg/mL and a clear, yellowish aqueous solution of HS-CDs was obtained as stock solution for the following experiments.

Brown agglomerates of HS-CDs/Ag emerged after dripping the flocculants and quencher of Ag+ (AgNO3) into 1 mL stock solution of HS-CDs. The agglomerates were then centrifuged at 5, 000 rpm for 10 min and eluted with 10 mL DW three times through repeated ultrasonic treatment and centrifuging. Different pH levels of PBS were used to dissolve the agglomerate to obtain the optimum sensor solution. Our preliminary study demonstrated that the agglomerate was difficult to dissolve in acidic solution and that an alkaline buffer solution offered better dissolution of the agglomerate. A higher pH would induce precipitation of AgOH and HgO, which was unfavorable for sensing. A weak alkaline PBS buffer (0.01 mol/L, 100 mL) with pH 7.4 was chosen as the optimum buffer solution to dissolve the agglomerate of HS-CDs/Ag being used as the sensing system [39].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a JEOL-S4800 Field Emission Transmission Electron Microscope (FESEM, JEOL, Japan), and the particle size was calculated with Nano Measurer 1.2.5 software (Fudan University, China). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was performed on a D8 ADVANCE powder X-ray diffract meter (Bruker Corp, Germany). Ultraviolet visible spectrum (UV–vis) absorption spectra were characterized by a UV–vis 2500 Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra used to confirm groups were performed on a Vetex 70 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Bruker Corp, Germany). Fluorescent spectra were measured on a LS-55 FL spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) with excitation and emission slit widths of 5 nm. The emission spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 300–600 nm at the excitation wavelength of 355 nm. All samples for FL measurements were performed in triplicate for statistical analysis. The amalgam was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and the data were collected using an Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, UK) equipped with an Al-Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). Prodigy7 inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) (Leeman Labs, USA) equipped with cold vapor technique was employed to detect Hg2+ in sample waters.

For fluorescent detection of Hg2+, 0.1 mL Hg2+ in varying concentrations (0–2.00 nmol/L) was added to 0.9 mL HS-CDs/Ag sensor, and the FL emission spectra were collected. The FL recovery factor ( F/F
0
- 1 ) versus the concentrations of Hg2+ data were used to calculate regression equation according to Eq. (1). In order to evaluate the selectivity of the sensor toward Hg2+, 1 mL of 17 common metal ions (Mg2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Co2+, Mn2+ Fe2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Cr6+, Ag+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Na+ and K+) with a 100-fold concentration of 0.10 nmol/L versus Hg2+ was separately added to 2 mL HS-CDs/Ag sensor. In order to evaluate the anti-interference of the sensor, 0.10 nmol/L of Hg2+ and 16 kinds of metal ions with the concentration of 0.01 mmol/L were dripped into the sensor.
(1)
F /
F
0
- 1 = a
[
C
]
+ b
Where F
0
and F were the FL intensities of the HS-CDs/Ag sensor at emission wavelength of 425 nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. C was the concentration of Hg2+.

To demonstrate the feasibility of the sensor for Hg2+ detection in practical applications, real water samples collected from a local river and tap water were centrifuged at 6, 000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles and were filtered through a 0.22 μm PES membrane. Then 0.1 mL water samples were spiked with standard solutions (0, 0.20, and 0.30 nmol/L), and the mixed solutions were evaluated by the proposed method. The FL intensities were measured, and the results were subjected to statistical analyses (n = 3) using Minitab 16.2.3 software (Minitab Inc., USA). A student's t-test was applied for significant differences (P < 0.05) between the proposed method and ICP-AES.

Surface status and size are critical factors in determining the FL of the QDs [40]. The TEM image of the HS-CDs in Fig. 2 A shows that the HS-CDs were spherical particles and the inset shows that the HS-CDs were an average particle size of 3.67 ± 1.29 nm in the range of 0.83–6.72 nm. The XRD pattern (Fig. S1) showed two diffraction peaks centered at ∼ 13.22° and ∼32.52°. The broad peak of ∼32.52° was attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms. This indicates that the carbon-based materials presented graphene-like structures which were similar to those of sulfur and nitrogen doped CDs. FTIR spectra of CA, L-cys and HS-CDs are shown in Fig. 2B. The peak at ∼3244 cm−1 was assigned to O-H stretching vibrations, the peaks at ∼3450 cm−1 and ∼3050 cm−1 were assigned to N-H stretching vibrations of primary amine, and the peak at ∼2610 cm−1 was assigned to S-H stretching vibrations [38]. The peaks at ∼1728 cm−1 and ∼1418 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of C=O and C-O of carbonyl on the HS-CDs. The peak at 1207 cm−1 was assigned to C-N stretching vibrations of amide. The FTIR spectrum illustrated that the HS-CDs were successfully functionalized with sulfhydryl moieties (-SH) [25,35], carboxylic (-COOH) and amino (-NH2) moieties. The functional groups and chemical composition of the HS-CDs were examined by XPS (Fig. 2C). The four peaks centered at 283.56 eV, 401.47 eV, 530.0 eV and 164.71 eV could be attributed to C1s, N1s, O1s and S1s respectively. The C1s spectrum of the HS-CDs (Fig. S2A) could be de-convoluted into four surface contents: C-O-C (288.77 eV), C=O (286.47 eV), C-N (284.97 eV), and C-S (284.77 eV). Two peaks at 531.69 eV and 533.06 eV in the O1s spectrum (Fig. S2B) were ascribed to C=O and C-O-C. The N1s spectrum (Fig. S2C) showed two peaks at 401.27 eV and 401.77 eV, attributed to N-C and N-H. The S2p spectrum (Fig. S2D) revealed the presence of C-S-C units. The XPS results further confirmed that the HS-CDs were functionalized with -COOH, -NH2 and -SH [22–25].

The UV–vis absorption spectra and FL excitation/emission spectra of the HS-CDs are presented in Fig. 2D. The peak at ∼242 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum was attributed to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [23,41]. Another peak at ∼360 nm was derived from n-π* transition of C=O and n-σ* transition of heteroatom groups like -SH, -OH and -NH2 [42,43]. These active groups offered high solubility for HS-CDs in aqueous solution and changed the surface state of the HS-CDs to enhance quantum yield. These groups could easily chelate silver ions resulting in FL quenching [43] and readily induce aggregation resulting in precipitates. Fig. 2D also indicates that the HS-CDs had the optimal FL excitation and emission wavelengths at 355 nm and 425 nm respectively. The HS-CDs showed bright blue FL under UV lamp (inset of Fig. 2D right). Fig. 2E shows that the FL emission of the HS-CDs presented a very slight spectra shift when excited by different excitation wavelengths from 310 nm to 380 nm. This suggested a stable excitation-independent emission behavior of the HS-CDs which was favorable in sensing. Fig. 2F demonstrates the influence of pH on the FL of HS-CDs. The FL exhibited excellent stability in a wide pH range (pH 5–11) due to the deprotonation equilibrium of -NH3 + and -COOH.

Brown precipitates were obtained when Ag+ was dripped into the stock solution of HS-CDs owing to the agglomeration of HS-CDs/Ag. Fig. 3 A shows that the absorption peak of HS-CDs at 360 nm disappeared after dripping Ag+ into the solution. This suggested the agglomeration of cross-linking HS-CDs/Ag with FL quenching due to chelation between Ag+ and -SH, -COOH and -NH2 on HS-CDs. The insets of Fig. 3A are corresponding photographs of agglomerates observed under visual light (a) and UV lamp at 365 nm (b). In order to obtain the appropriate ratio of Ag+ and HS-CDs to form the HS-CDs/Ag agglomerate different volumes of Ag+ were dripped into HS-CDs. Fig. S3 shows that the amount of the agglomerates increased and the FL of the HS-CDs was more strongly quenched with increasing concentrations of Ag+ from 0.001 to 0.200 mol/L in the same volume. The amount of agglomerates and the FL of the HS-CDs presented indistinct difference when 2 mL Ag+ with the concentrations of 0.1 and 0.2 mol/L were dripped into the stock solution. Therefore 0.1 mol/L Ag+ was chosen as flocculant and quencher to produce agglomerates. XPS spectrum of the HS-CDs/Ag (refer to Fig. 2C) showed that the three peaks centered at 398.40 eV, 368.17 eV, 162.57 eV could be ascribed to N1s, Ag3d and S2p respectively, indicating the presence of element Ag in the sensor. The spectrum of N1s could be de-convoluted into two surface contents (Fig. S4A): -N=O (396.20 eV) and N-Ag (393.55 eV). Compared to the N1s spectrum of the HS-CDs in Fig. S2C, the peak shift of N1s from 401.70 eV to 398.40 eV was observed because of the electronic screening from Ag+ with a higher electron-donating capacity. These results confirmed the coordination between -NH2 and Ag+ resulting in FL quenching.

Compared with the HS-CDs, the FTIR spectrum of the HS-CDs/Ag in Fig. 3B shows that the peak of -OH at 3244 cm−1 and the peak of -SH at 2610 cm−1 disappeared, the intensity of -NH2 peaks at 3050 and 3445 cm−1 decreased, and the peak of -COOH at 1418 cm−1 shifted to 1387 cm−1 after dripping Ag+ into HS-CDs. These changes suggested the coordination between Ag+ and -SH, -OH and -NH2, which reduced the vibration intensity of the S-H, O-H and N-H because increasing molecular weight decreased the dipole moment of these active groups [44,45]. Ag+ provided two binding sites for a complex unit and the prior reaction between Ag+ and -SH formed linear geometry [-S-Ag-S-] to achieve silver-thiolate precipitates [46]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of S2p (Fig. 3C) and Ag3d (Fig. 3D) further confirmed the presence of the S-Ag bond. Thereafter, the coordination between Ag+ and -NH2/-COOH randomly formed -S-Ag-NH-, -NH-Ag-NH-, -S-Ag-CO- and -NH-Ag-CO-, which enhanced the cross-linking formation of bulk complexes resulting in precipitates. The precipitates were dissolved in 0.01 mmol/L PBS buffer with pH 7.4 (Fig. S5) to obtain the HS-CDs/Ag sensor (Fig. S6A left). Color fading was observed after dripping Hg2+ into the solution of HS-CDs/Ag (shown in Fig. S6A right). Fig. S6B shows the FL spectra of HS-CDs/Ag and HS-CDs/Ag + Hg2+ with implications that Hg2+ could induce FL recovery of the HS-CDs. Hence, the signal-on fluorescent sensor of HS-CDs/Ag could be developed for sensing Hg2+ based on the FL recovery.

Gradients of Hg2+ (0–2.00 nmol/L) were dripped into the HS-CDs/Ag sensor, and the FL of the sensor gradually recovered with an increasing concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 0–2.00 nmol/L, which was shown in Fig. 4 A. The FL intensity decreased when the concentration of Hg2+ was higher than 1.00 nmol/L. We suggested that the excessive Hg2+ coordinated with -NH2 on the surface of HS-CDs/Hg resulting in FL quenching. The plot of FL intensity vs concentrations of Hg2+ is presented in Fig. 4B, and the linear range is displayed in the inset. A good linear relationship between [ F/F
0
– 1 ] and concentrations of Hg2+ was obtained from 0.01 to 0.75 nmol/L with a correlation coefficient ( R
2
) of 0.9885. The linear equation is expressed as F/F
0
– 1 = 12.68 [C] + 1.8684 (nmol/L). The LOD was calculated to be 4.2 pmol/L, which was extremely lower than most of the reported nanosensors (Table S1) and CDs based sensors (Table S2). The sensor could respond to Hg2+ at a very low concentration of 0.015 nmol/L (LOQ), which was much lower than the EPA standard of 10 nmol/L.

The coordination capability between metal ions and -SH abided by the order of Hg2+ ≫ Ag+ > Cu2+ > Cd2+ > Zn2+ [35], which allowed Hg2+ to readily replace the Ag+ in HS-CDs/Ag to form new complexes of HS-CDs/Hg. FTIR was employed to reveal active group changes in the sensing system (refer to Fig. 3B). After dripping Hg2+ into the sensor, a distinct peak at ∼461 cm−1 of S-Hg bond appeared implying the substitution of Hg2+ for Ag+. Double peaks at ∼3586 cm−1 and ∼3526 cm−1 were ascribed to primary amine and the peak at 1614 cm−1 was assigned to shear vibration of N-H which was regarded as the restitution of -NH2. Because of the transformation of spatial conformation, the tetrahedron type of HS-CDs/Hg with a center of Hg2+ exhibited higher electron withdrawal than the discrete solution of the HS-CDs, which resulted in extreme enhancement of the dipole moment of -NH2. Therefor the primary amine peak showed a large shift over 100 nm to higher wavenumbers. The peak at 1207 cm−1 disappeared due to the fracture of C-N bonds during the formation of new complexes. The competitive coordination of Hg2+ and Ag+ toward -SH resulted in a new complex of HS-CDs/Hg, which was confirmed by the FTIR and XPS analyses. The spatial transformation from HS-CDs/Ag to HS-CDs/Hg caused fracture of the hydrophilic bonds and Ag+, resulting in exposure of hydrophilic groups, especially -NH2. The bonds of -S-Ag-NH-, -NH-Ag-NH- were likely broken during the transformation and led to exposure of -NH2, which offered energy traps for FL recovery [47]. The exposure of hydrophilic groups also improved the solubility of HS-CDs/Hg and changed the surface state of HS-CDs to further contribute to FL recovery of HS-CDs.

Generally, the replaced Ag+ in the sensing system and the excessively dripped Hg2+ might recombine with the exposed -NH2 of HS-CDs/Hg resulting in FL quenching again, but the facile and rapid generation of amalgam prevents the FL of HS-CDs/Hg from being re-quenched by free metal ions. The XPS spectrum of the HS-CDs/Ag + Hg2+ presented four peaks centered at 400.40 eV, 367.28 eV, 162.57 eV and 100.41 eV, which could be ascribed to N1s, Ag3d, S2p and Hg4f (refer to Fig. 1C). The spectrum of N1s could be de-convoluted into four surface contents: -N=O (407.20 eV), -NH2 (400.20 eV), -N=C (398.40 eV) and N-Hg (396.20 eV) (refer to Fig. S4B). Compared with the N1s spectrum of HS-CDs/Ag (Fig. S4A), the restitution of -NH2 was clearly observed. This was consistent with the FTIR results. The spectra implied that the chemical bond of N-Ag was replaced by the N-Hg during the spatial transformation. The de-convoluted spectrum of S-Hg (162.4 eV) in the S2p spectrum of the HS-CDs/Ag + Hg2+ illustrated the chemical binding between -SH and Hg2+, further validating the substitution of Hg2+ for Ag+. Comparing the XPS spectra of HS-CDs and HS-CDs/Ag (Fig. 5 A), the S2p spectrum of the HS-CDs/Ag + Hg2+ presented a prominent peak shift from 163.88 eV to 162.57 eV due to the decreasing binding energy of sulfur element induced by the higher electron donating of Hg2+ rather than Ag2+. The peaks at 376.17 and 109.12 eV in XPS spectrum of HS-CDs/Ag + Hg2+ (refer to Fig. 2C) confirmed the existence of Ag3d and Hg4f. The high resolution XPS spectra of Ag3d (Fig. 5B) and Hg4f (Fig. 5C) implied the presence of Ag2Hg3 (refer to Fig. 1) [48]. The results indicated that a rapid reaction had taken place between Ag+ from HS-CDs/Ag and Hg2+ to form amalgam [49,50]. This led to the absence of free Ag+ and free Hg2+ to coordinate with -NH2 again, and more -NH2 was exposed to provide energy traps to enhance FL recovery. Ag+ on HS-CDs/Ag was replaced by Hg2+ even at extremely low concentration, which was beneficial for the detection of Hg2+ even at trace levels. Comparing to the reported carbon based Hg2+ detectors, the HS-CDs/Ag sensor presented the lowest LOD (Table S2), indicating ultra-trace detection of Hg2+ with high sensitivity toward Hg2+. On the basis of the excellent FL recovery and the extremely low LOD, the unique signal-on FL sensor was shown to superbly detect trace amount of Hg2+ in aqueous solutions.

The column diagrams of FL recovery depicted in Fig. 6 A show that there was negligible FL recovery when dripping metal ions other than Hg2+ and Pb2+. The corresponding photographs under UV lamp (Fig. S7 right) indicated that obvious FL recovery could be observed by dripping Hg2+ and Pb2+ into the HS-CDs/Ag sensor. Even though Pb2+ could induce FL recovery of the sensor the recovery intensity was less than 30% of the Hg2+ recovered. Hg2+ induced color fading of the sensor (inset of Fig. S7 left) which could assist in the assessment of the sensor's selectivity toward Hg2+. The sensor clearly exhibited good selectivity toward Hg2+.

Metal ions co-existing with Hg2+ in natural environments were separately added to the Hg2+-containing matrices to further assess the selectivity and anti-interference of the HS-CDs/Ag sensor toward Hg2+. The interference was negligible (P > 0.05) except for Pb2+ (P < 0.05) with a slight FL enhancement (Fig. 6B). For the purpose of hazard assessment of Hg2+ there was no adverse effect on monitoring Hg2+ in aqueous solutions with Pb2+ co-existing. This series of experiments demonstrated insignificant interference by the co-existing metal ions on HS-CDs/Ag detection of Hg2+ and confirmed that the sensor possessed an acceptable selectivity toward Hg2+.

The response of the HS-CDs FL toward Hg2+ in the sensor detection range is shown in Fig. S8. The HS-CDs FL unobviously responded to Hg2+ when the concentration was lower than 1 nmol/L. The FL was quenched by Hg2+ when the concentration of Hg2+ was higher than 1 nM. In the detection range of the HS-CDs/Ag sensor the FL of HS-CDs showed slight differences with/without Hg2+ (Table S3).

The concentrations of Hg2+ determined by the signal-on fluorescent sensor are shown in Table 1 . Satisfactory recoveries of 94.57%–102.73% and acceptable RSDs of 1.34%–7.41% were obtained. The test results of the proposed method were in good agreement with ICP-AES (P > 0.05). All these results implied a practical, convenient, sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor for determination of Hg2+ in real water samples.

We reported an Ag+ mediated sulfhydryl functionalized carbon dots (HS-CDs) fluorescent sensor for determination of Hg2+ in aqueous solutions. The signal-on sensor can be facilely fabricated by introducing Ag+ into the HS-CDs solution to form agglomerates of HS-CDs/Ag with strong FL quenching and consequently dispersed in 0.01 mol/L pH7.4 PBS buffer as a FL sensor for Hg2+detection. Hg2+ could recover the FL of HS-CDs owing to exposure of -NH2 during the transformation from HS-CDs/Ag to HS-CDs/Hg based on the substitution of Hg2+ for Ag+. The rapid formation of amalgam reduced the replaced Ag+ and dripped Hg2+ in the system, which limited free metal ions to chelate the HS-CDs so as to avoid FL re-quenching. The affinity of -SH toward Hg2+ endowed the selectivity of the sensor. The rapid formation of amalgam accompanying the exposure of -NH2 on HS-CDs/Hg enhanced the sensitivity of the sensor. The applicability of the HS-CDs/Ag sensor for Hg2+ detection in real water samples was then demonstrated with exceptional performances, and the results were consistent with ICP-AES. The proposed sensor presented precision and accuracy for practical detection of Hg2+ with high sensibility and selectivity.
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31801628).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2019.04.052.
